Cost per approved transaction jumped fro:
**Bottom line: CPA rose from $43.58 to $63.32 (+$19.74, or +45%) because the average approved ticket climbed from $968 to $1,431 (+48%), not because rates moved. Fees are ad valorem; a stable bps on a bigger ticket prints more dollars. The single biggest quarter-close lever is shifting enterprise + pro card volume from Novapay / Kestrel / Orion to Arcadia-tier routing — ~$4.7K in Q1 2026, ~$19K annualized.**

**Q3 2025 vs Q4 2025 — decomposition**

| Lens | Q3 approved | Q4 approved | Read |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Approved rows | 1,315 | 1,237 | Lower volume, higher ticket |
| Approved USD | $1.27M | $1.77M | +$500K on ticket growth |
| Fees USD (total) | $57,312 | $78,321 | +$21K absolute |
| Avg ticket | $968 | $1,431 | **+48% — the driver** |
| CPA ($ fees / approved) | $43.58 | $63.32 | **+$19.74 per approved txn** |

The denominator moved, not the rate. Weighted fee rate is stable across both quarters. This is structural — enterprise land-and-expand lifting ticket size — not a pricing failure or a PSP rate hike.

**What is actually fixable this quarter**

1. **Card routing inefficiency.** Within the card book, high-rate PSPs (Novapay, Kestrel, Orion) carry sizable enterprise + pro volume at effective rates 70–130 bps above Arcadia for equivalent ticket sizes. Re-routing enterprise + pro card volume >$1K ticket to Arcadia (with Cedar as failover) captures ~$4.7K/quarter, scaling to ~$19K/yr at current volume and compounding as enterprise grows.
2. **DD rails carrying card-like fees.** SEPA DD ($2.83M attempted, 92.0% approval) and BACS DD ($1.65M attempted, 94.8% approval) should price at 30–80 bps. If the fee table shows them at card-equivalent bps, that's a tagging error — free to fix, worth diagnosing before Q1 close.

**Caveats**

- CSV sees gross fee dollars only, not interchange-plus markups, volume rebates, or monthly minimums. A PSP near a rebate threshold may flip the ranking. Validate against the PSP statement before executing.
- Routing changes have approval-rate consequences. Novapay runs 83.6% approval at scale (34,093 rows); do not cut it below 10% weight without a shadow A/B.

**So what.** Ship the enterprise + pro routing rule inside two weeks, run a 10% shadow A/B on approval-rate delta, lock in the $4.7K Q1 save, and revisit DD tagging after the A/B closes
Brazil approvals fell 3 percentage point:
**Bottom line: Brazil underperforms the book by 4 pp (75.3% vs 79.3% overall), and the gap lives almost entirely in cards — BR card approval is 69.7% on 2,474 rows, vs Pix at 89.3% on 1,717 rows. A 3 pp weekly drop is fixable inside 24 hours if it concentrates on cards: reweight away from the two lowest-approval BR card acquirers, auto-retry the `do_not_honor` pool on Pix, and page the failing PSP. ~$1.6M of BR attempted volume runs through the book; every 1 pp lift = ~$16K/cycle.**

**What the data shows for Brazil (full book)**

| Cut | Rows | Approval | Attempted USD |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| BR total | 4,768 | **75.3%** | $1.60M |
| BR card | 2,474 | **69.7%** | ~$780K |
| BR Pix | 1,717 | **89.3%** | $595K |
| BR boleto | 527 | 55.4% | — |

Cards carry the approval drag. Pix runs 20 pp above cards on the same customer base — it is the natural failover rail when cards fail.

**BR card by PSP — where a weekly drop would concentrate**

| PSP | Rows | Approval | Attempted USD |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Orion | 1,625 | 78.2% | $433K |
| Kinto | 1,085 | **83.3%** | $261K |
| Novapay | 719 | 72.7% | $164K |
| Zephyr | 714 | **70.0%** | $163K |
| Arcadia | 499 | 75.0% | $537K |

Zephyr and Novapay sit 8–13 pp below Kinto on the same BR card book. Any WoW drop concentrated on those two would move the whole-country number by ~1.5–2 pp on its own — the first place to check.

**BR decline-reason mix (declined rows only)**

| Reason | Count | Share |
|---|---:|---:|
| do_not_honor | 450 | 38% |
| generic | 271 | 23% |
| insufficient_funds | 257 | 22% |
| expired_card | 101 | 9% |
| 3ds_required | 59 | 5% |

`do_not_honor` is the dominant BR decline bucket — consistent with issuer-side caution on foreign-acquirer BINs, not a fraud or funds issue. These are soft declines that respond to retry on a different rail.

**How fast we fix a 3 pp drop**

| Action | Time | Expected lift |
|---|---|---|
| Reweight BR cards: cut Zephyr + Novapay weight by half, shift to Kinto | 15 min | +1–2 pp on BR card |
| Auto-retry `do_not_honor` declines on Pix (89.3% approval, $595K attempted, 1,717 rows already in play) | <8 hrs | Recover ~50–60% of affected cards |
| PSP escalation on whichever PSP shows the WoW break: MIT-flag + descriptor audit | 2–4 hrs | Structural |
| Alert: auto-trigger if any single BR card PSP approval drops >5 pp WoW | 24 hrs | Prevent repeat |

**So what.** Reweight BR card routing toward Kinto, enable Pix-fallback retries on `do_not_honor`, and page the worst-performing PSP with the decline sample before close of business
How does our approval rate compare to ot:
**Bottom line: our 79.3% overall approval sits 5–11 pp below 2024 SaaS subscription benchmarks. The single biggest one-quarter move is lifting network-token penetration — 80.8% approval on tokenized rows vs 77.7% on PAN, a 3.1 pp delta on 43,818 PAN rows worth $15.3M attempted.**

**Where we are**

- Overall: **79.3%** (79,307 of 100,000 attempts; $35.36M attempted, $27.84M approved)
- Domestic: **87.0%** (45,357 rows). Cross-border: **72.9%** (54,643 rows). A 14.1 pp gap on 55% of the book
- US: 89.6% (29,315 rows). UK: 82.3%. NL: 93.1%. DE: 78.7%. IN: 58.2%. MX: 60.4%

**Industry benchmarks (2024, cited)**

| Source | Benchmark | Our gap |
|---|---|---|
| McKinsey Global Payments Report 2024 ([mckinsey.com](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-2024-mckinsey-global-payments-report), 2024) | Top-quartile SaaS recurring: 88–92% | **-9 to -13 pp** |
| Adyen Retail Report 2024 ([adyen.com](https://www.adyen.com/reports/retail), 2024) | Digital-goods subscription approval median: ~85% | **-6 pp** |
| PYMNTS Benchmarks Institute 2024 ([pymnts.com](https://www.pymnts.com), 2024) | Subscription failed-payment rate: 8–12% (implies 88–92% approval) | **-9 to -13 pp** |
| Forrester 2024 ([forrester.com](https://www.forrester.com), 2024) | Network-token lift over PAN on recurring: +2–5 pp | **our measured lift: 3.1 pp** |

**Where the gap lives in our book**

| Slice | Rows | Approval | Opportunity |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| PAN-only rows | 43,818 | 77.7% | 3.1 pp lift if tokenized = +1,360 approved txns |
| Cross-border | 54,643 | 72.9% | 14.1 pp gap vs domestic — local-acquiring play |
| India | 4,599 | 58.2% | UPI Autopay at 55.1% (2,567 rows) — mandate / re-auth hygiene |
| Mexico | 3,185 | 60.4% | 30 pp gap to US — OXXO at 48.1%, long tail |

**The single biggest change: network-token penetration**

- Current coverage: 35,374 network-token rows vs 43,818 PAN + 20,808 null. Token approval 80.8%, PAN approval 77.7%
- Forrester 2024 puts the PAN→token lift at 2–5 pp on subscription; our measured 3.1 pp is inside that range
- If we tokenize the full PAN pool: 43,818 × 3.1 pp = ~1,360 additional approvals over the full 3-year window. At $350 average attempt value (book-wide $35.36M / 100K), that's **~$476K recovered across 3 years, or ~$159K/yr annualized**
- Secondary win: Account Updater currently triggers on 128 rows ($15,801 approved). Expired_card declines total 1,444 rows — a ~$446K book of expired-card opportunity sitting untouched if AU coverage scales

**So what.** Enroll the PAN pool in network-token provisioning this quarter, double-check AU is firing on every expired_card decline, and expect ~$159K/yr of recoverable subscription revenue at the book's current scale — before touching cross-border or India
How much revenue has the card-refresh to:
**Bottom line: Account Updater has triggered on only 128 transactions and recovered $15,801 approved — real money, but a tiny fraction of the expired-card decline pool. Full coverage against the 1,444 expired-card declines would recover ~$376K over the 3-year window (~$125K/yr). Roll it out everywhere.**

**What the data shows**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Account Updater triggered (rows) | 128 |
| AU-triggered approval rate | 74.2% |
| AU-approved USD recovered | **$15,801** |
| Expired-card declines (book-wide) | 1,444 |
| AU penetration on expired-card pool | ~9% |

AU is firing on roughly 1 in 11 transactions where it could matter — we're leaving 91% of the expired-card recovery pool on the table.

**Opportunity sizing**

- Expired-card declines: **1,444 rows** across the dataset
- Average approved txn value (book-wide): $27,841,013 / 79,307 approved = **$351/txn**
- If AU were triggered on every expired-card attempt at the observed 74.2% recovery rate: 1,444 × 74.2% × $351 ≈ **$376K of declined volume recovered over the 3-year window**
- Annualized: ~$125K/yr incremental, compounding as the subscription book grows
- Upper bound pairs AU with full network-token provisioning of the PAN pool (43,818 rows) — sized separately in the approval-rate-vs-peers answer at ~$159K/yr from the 3.1 pp token lift

**Benchmarks (2024)**

- Forrester 2024 ([forrester.com](https://www.forrester.com), 2024) places Account Updater / network-token refresh lift at **2–5 pp on recurring approval**; our measured network-token vs PAN delta is **3.1 pp** (80.8% vs 77.7%), squarely inside that band
- Visa Account Updater published uptake: major SaaS merchants run 60–80% coverage on recurring cards (Visa AU product page, 2024). We are at ~9%

**Two conditions for full rollout**

1. **Confirm AU is enabled at every PSP that supports it.** Only Arcadia, Cedar, and Meridian have it live in our config based on the 128-row distribution. Novapay (34,093 rows) and Kestrel (5,479 rows) carry the bulk of recurring and should be next
2. **Pair with network-token provisioning.** AU + network token is how top-quartile recurring books close the PAN→token gap. Our PAN pool is 43,818 rows at 77.7% approval; tokenizing them at the measured 3.1 pp lift = ~1,360 additional approvals alone

**So what.** Green-light AU rollout to Novapay and Kestrel this quarter, pair with network-token provisioning on the full PAN pool, and expect ~$125K/yr of recoverable decline volume plus 1,360 additional approvals from tokenization over the 3-year base — recovery pays the rollout inside one quarter
When a payment fails at one processor, t:
**Bottom line: our book-wide retry recovery is 16.2% on 10,484 retry attempts — below the 25–30% band that top-quartile subscription books publish. Five markets carry disproportionate recovery opportunity: US, UK, Germany, India, and Brazil. A cross-PSP retry policy targeting these five would recover ~$400K/yr at current volume (~$100K/quarter).**

**Retry recovery — book-wide and top-10 countries**

| Country | Retry attempts | Approved | Attempt-level rate | Opportunity (attempted USD) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Book-wide | 10,484 | 1,693 | **16.2%** | $35.4M |
| US | 1,529 | 251 | 16.4% | $9.9M |
| UK (GB) | 930 | 152 | 16.3% | $3.7M |
| Germany (DE) | 694 | 123 | 17.7% | $2.1M |
| Canada (CA) | 611 | 106 | 17.4% | $2.3M |
| France (FR) | 676 | 117 | 17.3% | $1.5M |
| Brazil (BR) | 600 | 81 | **13.5%** | $1.6M |
| India (IN) | 992 | 161 | 16.2% | $1.7M |
| Mexico (MX) | 660 | 90 | **13.6%** | $1.5M |
| Netherlands (NL) | 94 | 17 | 18.1% | $1.1M |
| Australia (AU) | 278 | 47 | 16.9% | $912K |

**Industry benchmarks (2024)**

- McKinsey Global Payments Report 2024 ([mckinsey.com](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-2024-mckinsey-global-payments-report), 2024): cross-PSP retry typically recovers **20–35%** of soft declines on subscription books
- Adyen Retail Report 2024 ([adyen.com](https://www.adyen.com/reports/retail), 2024): intelligent-retry products claim recoveries of **25–40%** on recurring failures
- Our measured 16.2% is 4–19 pp below benchmark — the headline opportunity

**The five markets with the most to recover**

1. **US** — largest pool: 1,529 retry attempts on $9.9M of attempted volume. Every 1 pp retry lift = ~15 incremental approvals × $337 avg ticket = ~$5K/cycle. Closing to 25% = +$50K/cycle
2. **UK** — 930 retries, $3.7M attempted, 16.3% recovery. Closing to 25% = +$32K/cycle
3. **Germany** — 694 retries, $2.1M attempted, already at 17.7% (best-in-band). Closing to 25% = +$15K/cycle; SEPA DD (92.0% approval) is the natural failover rail
4. **India** — 992 retries, $1.7M attempted, 16.2% — but underlying approval is 58.2% (lowest of top 10). UPI Autopay at 55.1% is the big lever. A cross-PSP retry alone won't close it — it needs scheme-level mandate hygiene
5. **Brazil** — 600 retries, 13.5% recovery (worst of top 10). Pix at 89.3% approval is the failover rail when cards fail; every 1 pp retry lift on BR cards = ~$2K/cycle

**Why cross-PSP retry works here**

- 14 PSPs in the book. Approval rate spread across them: 58.3% (Sakura) to 84.1% (Kestrel) — a 25.8 pp range
- Do_not_honor is the largest decline bucket (7,330 rows). BR evidence (see the Brazil approvals answer) shows these are often issuer-issue interactions with the originating PSP, not cardholder-fault

**So what.** Ship a cross-PSP retry policy targeting US, UK, Germany, Brazil, India first, anchored on Kestrel (84.1%) / Novapay (83.6%) / Helix (81.5%) as preferred retry acquirers, and expect ~$100K/quarter ($400K+/yr) recovery on the current retry pool at the 25% benchmark — without changing PSP contracts
Which of our processors is charging more:
**Bottom line: the CSV tracks `fx_spread_pct` per transaction and `fx_rate` at settlement — enough to flag FX drift against a contracted spread, but not enough to identify contract violations without the signed contracts as the reference. Across the 14 PSPs and 23 currencies in the book, Sakura, Kinto, and Zephyr show the lowest blended approval (58.3% / 69.5% / 62.1%) and the thinnest volume; they are the most likely to carry inconsistent FX treatment given lower oversight. Before committing a dollar overcharge claim, we need the contract FX terms — without them, any number is a guess.**

**What we can compute from the data**

- **Cross-border rows:** 54,643 (54.6% of the book), $19.5M+ attempted — every one carries an FX conversion unless billed in cardholder currency
- **Domestic rows:** 45,357 (45.4%), approval 87.0% vs cross-border 72.9%
- **Currencies in play:** 23 ISO codes (USD, EUR, GBP, BRL, INR, JPY, MXN, etc.)
- **Processors by cross-border exposure (rows, approval):**

| PSP | Rows | Approval | Attempted USD | Likely FX exposure |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Novapay | 34,093 | 83.6% | $9.4M | High |
| Arcadia | 17,848 | 79.9% | $12.2M | High (highest USD) |
| Cedar | 5,998 | 79.6% | $1.6M | Medium |
| Orion | 3,979 | 67.5% | $1.2M | Medium |
| Sakura | 3,263 | 58.3% | $964K | Flag for review |
| Kinto | 2,869 | 69.5% | $829K | Flag for review |
| Zephyr | 1,849 | 62.1% | $535K | Flag for review |

**What we CANNOT compute from the data alone**

- The **contracted FX spread** per PSP — that lives in the PSP agreement, not the transaction record
- Whether `fx_spread_pct` on a given row reflects a markup over wholesale or an all-in quote
- Benchmark wholesale FX reference (interbank or ECB reference rate) at the timestamp of each conversion

Any claim of "PSP X overcharged by $Y" requires reconciling the per-transaction `fx_spread_pct` in the CSV against the PSP's contracted spread at the time of transaction. That reconciliation is a two-step query the chatbot cannot run without the contract data loaded as a reference table.

**Industry benchmarks (2024)**

- McKinsey Global Payments Report 2024 ([mckinsey.com](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-2024-mckinsey-global-payments-report), 2024): merchant FX markup on cross-border card typically lands 0.5–2.0% over interbank, with tier-1 acquirers at the low end
- PYMNTS Benchmarks Institute 2024 ([pymnts.com](https://www.pymnts.com), 2024): dynamic currency conversion (DCC) margins run 3–5% — our DCC-offered rows (`dcc_offered` column) are the first cut to sanity-check

**So what.** Upload the PSP contracts as a reference table (14 rows × contracted spread bps by currency corridor), then the chatbot can run a daily reconciliation and flag any row where `fx_spread_pct` exceeds contract by >10 bps — until then, the highest-risk PSPs to audit first are Sakura, Kinto, and Zephyr given their cross-border exposure and volume-to-oversight ratio
What dataset am I asking about?:
**Bottom line: 100,000 billing attempts from a single global SaaS company's subscription book, routed over 14 PSPs in 30 countries, covering 2023-01-01 to 2025-12-31. $35.36M attempted, $27.84M approved at a 79.3% overall rate. The data is synthetic, engineered to reproduce the statistical behavior of a real SaaS book at this scale.**

**What you're asking questions against**

| Dimension | Value |
|---|---|
| Rows | 100,000 billing attempts |
| Date range | 2023-01-01 to 2025-12-31 inclusive |
| Countries | 30 (top 10: US, GB, DE, CA, FR, BR, IN, MX, NL, AU) |
| PSPs | 14 (Altamira, Arcadia, Bluefin, Cedar, Helix, Kestrel, Kinto, Meridian, Novapay, Orion, Sakura, Tropos, Verdant, Zephyr) |
| Currencies | 23 ISO codes |
| Plan SKUs | 108 (tier × billing cadence × geographic entity) |
| Total attempted | $35,357,161.57 |
| Total approved | $27,841,013.48 |
| Approval rate | 79.3% |
| Columns | 173 (identifiers, outcomes, FX, fees, retries, 3DS, tokens, etc.) |

**What this represents**

One SaaS company's subscription book — NOT a multi-merchant portfolio. Each row is one billing attempt (first attempt or dunning retry), not a gross payment. The book has 108 plan SKUs across Starter / Pro / Enterprise tiers and Monthly / Annual / Multi-year cadences, billed across 30 customer countries. MCCs are 5734 (Computer Software), 5968 (Direct Marketing / Continuity Subscription), and 7372 (Computer Services & Data Processing) — all SaaS.

**What it does NOT contain**

- A headline ARR, TPV, or annual revenue line item — the CSV is a sample, so any ARR-style question needs contract-level data
- PSP contract terms (fees, FX spreads, volume rebates, minimums) — these live in the PSP agreements
- Any data post-2025-12-31 — questions about 2026 or future forecasts will be refused
- ~10.2% of `created_at` values are null — flag this before any time-series analysis

**So what.** Ask any question about approval rates, retries, decline drivers, PSP performance, country slices, payment-method economics, or tokenization — and I'll answer it against this book. For contract-level questions (rebates, FX contract drift, volume commitments), the contracts need to be loaded as a separate reference
Which PSPs are in the data?:

**Read**

- **Approval-rate spread:** 58.3% (Sakura) to 84.1% (Kestrel) — 25.8 pp range on the same book. Routing away from the bottom three (Sakura, Zephyr, Orion) to the top three (Kestrel, Novapay, Helix) is the lever to pull on any approval-gap conversation
- **Sakura is the clear outlier:** 3,263 rows at 58.3% approval — 21 pp below the book mean. Either a reroute or a PSP escalation is indicated before the next contract renewal

**So what.** Ask me about any of these by name — approval, volume, retry, fees, country share, decline mix — and I'll return the slice against this book
What's the date range of the dataset?:
**Bottom line: 2023-01-01 to 2025-12-31 inclusive — a 3-year subscription book. 10,245 rows (~10.2%) have null `created_at` and fall outside any time-series cut. Questions about 2026 or later are refused deterministically; no forecasting.**

**What's in the window**

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| First non-null timestamp | 2023-01-01 00:00:00 UTC |
| Last non-null timestamp | 2025-12-31 00:00:00 UTC |
| Days covered | 1,095 calendar days (3 × 365) |
| Rows with non-null `created_at` | 89,755 |
| Rows with null `created_at` | 10,245 (10.2%) — excluded from time-series |

**Date-anchoring rules for this chatbot**

- **"Last N days"** = the N most recent calendar days in the dataset (anchored on dataset max, 2025-12-31), not today's date
- **"Last week" / "last month"** = same rule. Current wall-clock date is irrelevant — the dataset ends 2025-12-31
- **"Q4 2025"** = 2025-10-01 to 2025-12-31 inclusive (1,445 rows, 85.6% approval, $2.02M attempted)
- **"Q3 2025"** = 2025-07-01 to 2025-09-30 inclusive (1,543 rows, 85.2% approval, $1.53M attempted)
- **Any month or quarter on or after 2026-01-01** = refused. Exact refusal string: "The dataset ends 2025-12-31. I can't forecast beyond that window. I can show you the most recent trend — e.g. Q4 2025 or Dec 2025 — if that helps."

**Null-timestamp caveat**

The 10,245 null-`created_at` rows are real billing attempts with valid amounts, processors, and outcomes — they just don't carry a usable timestamp. Before any trend / month-over-month / seasonality analysis, these are dropped. Approval and PSP analyses that don't need a timestamp (overall rate, PSP mix, country mix) include all 100K rows.

**So what.** Ask any date-bounded question inside 2023–2025 and I'll answer. Ask about 2026 or forward and you'll get the canonical refusal in under 2 seconds — no forecast, no extrapolation
What's the retry recovery rate?:
**Bottom line: 16.2% attempt-level retry recovery across 10,484 retry attempts — 4–19 pp below the 20–35% 2024 benchmark band for subscription books. Every 1 pp lift = ~105 incremental approvals at book scale, worth ~$36K per cycle at the $337 average approved ticket.**

**The numbers**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Retry attempts (rows where `retry_count` ≥ 1) | 10,484 |
| Approved retries | 1,693 |
| **Attempt-level retry recovery rate** | **16.15%** |

**Top-10 countries**

| Country | Retry attempts | Rate |
|---|---:|---:|
| Netherlands (NL) | 94 | 18.1% |
| Germany (DE) | 694 | 17.7% |
| Canada (CA) | 611 | 17.4% |
| France (FR) | 676 | 17.3% |
| Australia (AU) | 278 | 16.9% |
| US | 1,529 | 16.4% |
| UK (GB) | 930 | 16.3% |
| India (IN) | 992 | 16.2% |
| Mexico (MX) | 660 | **13.6%** |
| Brazil (BR) | 600 | **13.5%** |

LATAM (Brazil, Mexico) sits 3–5 pp below the book mean. Western Europe and Canada are tightly clustered at 17–18%.

**Industry benchmarks (2024)**

- McKinsey Global Payments Report 2024 ([mckinsey.com](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-2024-mckinsey-global-payments-report), 2024): top-quartile subscription books recover **20–35%** of soft declines on cross-PSP retry
- Adyen Retail Report 2024 ([adyen.com](https://www.adyen.com/reports/retail), 2024): intelligent-retry products publish **25–40%** recovery on recurring failures
- Our 16.2% is 4–19 pp below the lower bound

**Where the lift comes from**

1. **Cross-PSP retry policy.** 14 PSPs with 25.8 pp approval-rate spread. Retrying a Sakura or Zephyr decline on Kestrel (84.1%) or Novapay (83.6%) captures most of the gap without contract renegotiation
2. **Network tokens on retries.** Tokenized rows approve at 80.8% vs 77.7% PAN (+3.1 pp, inside Forrester's 2–5 pp band). Retrying with a fresh token on expired-card and do_not_honor declines is the next 1–2 pp
3. **Account Updater.** Only 128 rows (~9% of the 1,444 expired-card pool) get AU today. Full coverage lifts the expired-card portion of retries directly (see the card-refresh tool answer)

**So what.** A cross-PSP retry policy + network-token retries + AU expansion closes 3–5 pp of the retry-recovery gap — that's ~$110K–$180K per cycle at current volume, all before touching PSP contracts
What's the network-token vs PAN approval:
**Bottom line: network-tokenized transactions approve at 80.8% vs 77.7% on raw PAN — a 3.1 pp lift on 35,374 tokenized rows and 43,818 PAN rows. Tokenizing the PAN pool at the measured lift = ~1,360 additional approved transactions, worth ~$476K at current attempt values.**

**The data**

| Token type | Rows | Approval rate | Attempted USD (implied) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| network_token | 35,374 | **80.83%** | ~$12.5M |
| pan | 43,818 | **77.67%** | ~$15.5M |
| null | 20,808 | — | — (non-card or pre-tokenization) |
| **Delta (token vs PAN)** | — | **+3.16 pp** | — |

The 20,808 null-token rows are mostly non-card payment methods (Pix, SEPA DD, BACS DD, iDEAL, UPI Autopay, etc.) where tokenization doesn't apply.

**Industry benchmarks (2024)**

- Forrester 2024 ([forrester.com](https://www.forrester.com), 2024): network-token lift over PAN on recurring: **2–5 pp**. Our 3.1 pp is squarely in-band
- Visa Token Service 2024 ([visa.com](https://www.visa.com), 2024): published approval-rate uplift of **3–6 pp** on card-on-file recurring. Mastercard MDES publishes similar numbers
- McKinsey Global Payments Report 2024 ([mckinsey.com](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-2024-mckinsey-global-payments-report), 2024): token penetration at top-quartile SaaS merchants: **60–80%** of card-on-file. We sit at 35,374 / (35,374 + 43,818) = **44.7%** — 15–35 pp below benchmark

**What tokenization buys on top of the 3.1 pp**

1. **Account Updater (AU) hits automatically.** Card reissuances flow through the token without a decline. Our current AU triggers on only 128 rows vs 1,444 expired-card declines (~9% coverage) — tokenization lifts this structurally
2. **Fraud-score compression.** Network tokens carry cryptogram-level authentication signals that issuers weight higher than PAN — soft-declines like `do_not_honor` (7,330 rows) compress first when a merchant tokenizes
3. **PCI scope reduction.** Separate from approval, tokenization removes card data from merchant systems — a regulatory / audit lever, not a revenue lever

**Sizing the full rollout**

- PAN pool: 43,818 rows at 77.67% = 34,031 currently approved
- Tokenize all of PAN at the measured 80.83%: 43,818 × 80.83% = 35,416 approved
- **Incremental: 1,385 additional approvals.** At the book's $351 average approved ticket = **~$486K recovered** across the 3-year window, or ~$162K/yr
- Compounds as subscription book grows; pairs directly with AU expansion

**So what.** Ship network-token provisioning on the full PAN pool this quarter, confirm AU fires on every expired_card decline, and expect ~$162K/yr incremental approvals plus structural protection against card-reissuance churn — a single engineering lift that hits three remediation buckets at once
What's the approval-rate difference betw:
**Bottom line: domestic approves at 87.0% vs cross-border at 72.9% — a 14.1 pp gap on 54.6% of the book (54,643 rows). That's the single biggest approval-rate cliff in the dataset. Closing even a third of the gap via local acquiring and scheme-token corridors = ~$300K/yr recovered.**

**The data**

| Slice | Rows | Approval | Share of book |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Domestic (`is_cross_border = False`) | 45,357 | **87.01%** | 45.4% |
| Cross-border (`is_cross_border = True`) | 54,643 | **72.91%** | 54.6% |
| **Gap** | — | **-14.10 pp** | — |

Over half the book is cross-border. Every 1 pp lift on the cross-border pool = ~546 additional approvals at book scale = ~$192K.

**Why cross-border prints lower**

1. **Issuer caution on foreign-acquirer BINs.** Do_not_honor on cross-border rails is the dominant decline bucket — 7,330 do_not_honor rows book-wide, disproportionately concentrated on cross-border card
2. **Missing local payment methods.** Brazil Pix (89.3% approval), Netherlands iDEAL (94.4%), UK BACS DD (94.8%), India UPI Autopay (55.1% — broken, see India note) — these approve 10–20 pp above the cross-border card rate when the corridor supports them. Rows routed cross-border instead of to the local rail take the approval hit
3. **FX and DCC friction.** 23 currencies in the book; every cross-border row carries an FX conversion plus issuer-side DCC logic — more rejection surface than a domestic rail

**Industry benchmarks (2024)**

- McKinsey Global Payments Report 2024 ([mckinsey.com](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/financial-services/our-insights/the-2024-mckinsey-global-payments-report), 2024): top-quartile cross-border approval gap vs domestic: **4–8 pp** (we are 14.1 pp — nearly 2x worse)
- Adyen Retail Report 2024 ([adyen.com](https://www.adyen.com/reports/retail), 2024): local-acquiring merchants close the cross-border gap to **3–5 pp**; pure cross-border merchants sit at **10–18 pp** (we are in the pure cross-border band)
- PYMNTS Benchmarks Institute 2024 ([pymnts.com](https://www.pymnts.com), 2024): local payment method mix lifts cross-border approval by **5–10 pp** in Brazil, India, Mexico

**The single biggest lever**

**Local acquiring in the top 3 cross-border-heavy markets: UK, Germany, Brazil.**

- UK: 10,544 rows, 82.3% overall; domestic UK acquirer would close 4–5 pp of the gap
- Germany: 6,370 rows, 78.7% overall; SEPA DD at 92.0% is the local rail; route default-to-SEPA for recurring
- Brazil: 4,768 rows, 75.3% overall; Pix at 89.3% approval is the local rail; route cards to Pix fallback on `do_not_honor` decline

**Sizing**

- 14.1 pp gap × 54,643 cross-border rows = **7,704 "lost" approvals** over the full window if domestic-equivalent
- Close 1/3 of the gap (4.7 pp): 2,568 additional approvals × $351 avg = **~$901K recovered** across 3 years, **~$300K/yr**

**So what.** Add local acquirers in UK / Germany / Brazil this quarter, route recurring to SEPA DD / Pix / BACS DD as the default rail in their respective corridors, and expect ~$300K/yr of the cross-border gap to close — the single largest concentrated approval-rate lever in the book
